data.title: HODL: Bitcoin's Soars To A Multi-Month High Amid Quiet Relative Strength (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Bitcoin flashed across investors’ radars on Wednesday, April 22. The world’s most valuable cryptocurrency scaled $79,000 for the first time since early February as a risk-on appetite permeated markets, despite Brent crude oil flirting with $100 per barrel. Ongoing uncertainty regarding the conflict in Iran once weighed on crypto, but now it seems to be a significantly smaller factor... maybe even a bullish one.

Today, I’m analyzing the VanEck Bitcoin ETF (HODL). With bitcoin pacing for its best close in months, I remain bullish on the token. Today, I’ll offer a fresh look at bitcoin technicals, performance across timeframes, fundamentals from an intermarket perspective, and a seasonal assessment.

Bitcoin Jumps to $79,000

According to the issuer, HODL seeks to track the performance of its Trust. The Trust's investment objective is to track the price of bitcoin, less the expenses of the Trust's operations. The Trust is a passive investment vehicle that does not seek to generate returns beyond tracking bitcoin's price.

HODL is a small ETF, now with just $1.3 billion in assets under management as of April 22. Its annual expense ratio is low at only 20 basis points, while there is no trailing 12-month dividend yield. For full disclosure, I own the iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT) in my taxable account (a 1.2% portfolio position), however I see HOLD and IBIT as quite comparable.

Share-price momentum is rated poorly by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system, currently at an F ETF Grade in that category. I’ll note later, however, that bitcoin actually leads the global market in recent weeks, so the technicals and relative momentum are better than prospective investors may realize.

Crypto Outperforming

As far as risk is concerned, HOLD’s historical realized volatility is high at 38%, but it has certainly been higher in its brief history. Even with April 22’s best day since early March, implied volatility is in check at 42%. Liquidity is also strong with HODL. Its average daily volume is north of 2.3 million shares, while the median 30-day bid/ask spread is tight at 5 basis points.

Keeping me upbeat about bitcoin (and bitcoin ETFs, including HODL) is its sudden relative strength surge. Now 30% off its February nadir, HOLD is +17% on a default 2-month zoom, which beats the S&P 500 ETF’s (SPY) 3.2% total return. Bitcoin’s gains have been gold’s pain. The yellow metal is –7.4% in the past 2 months, as measured by the SPDR Gold Trust ETF (GLD).

All the while, bitcoin sentiment remains lackluster, so I would not be surprised to see HOLD continue to close the proverbial (and technical) alligator jaws to precious metals.

HODL +30% from the February Low

How much room is there to run on bitcoin? Well, at the very least, it could climb from the bottom slot on Goldman Sachs ’ total return leaderboard. A rally just to the middle section in the chart below would imply significant gains over the months ahead.

Bitcoin: Bottom of the Pack YTD

Speaking of the calendar, bitcoin has historically performed well from April through July. When scanning returns since the cryptocurrency’s inception, the current and out-month is the best pair on the calendar, save October-November. It’s prudent not to look at average returns, since the 2010-2013 period can distort the return picture.

Hence, I prefer to gauge median monthly returns. There, we find that the April through July gains have been 11.5%, 6.7%, 3.8%, and 8.9%, respectively. So, seasonality is a friend to the “hodlers.”

Bitcoin: Bullish Trends Through July

The Technical Take

With improved absolute and relative strength and bullish calendar trends, let’s call out specific bitcoin price levels to watch. Of course, with extremely low tracking error, investors can use this assessment to make informed risk management allocation choices for their HODL stakes.

Notice in the chart below that bitcoin has rallied to its best levels since early February. The ascent comes alongside a steep rise in the RSI momentum indicator at the top of the graph. That’s bullish confirmation between price and momentum, which is a positive. What’s not ideal is the long-term 200-day moving average, as it is declining, suggesting that the bears control the primary trend.

Also take a look at the familiar bear flag pattern that we saw in November through the turn of the year. The current formation bears watching. Key will be for bitcoin to rally through the November low of $80,600. Above that, there’s a confluence between the 200dma and the 38.2% Fibonacci retracement of the October to February plunge.

So, it’s by no means is an all clear, but there are signs that the low from more than two months ago is durable.

Bitcoin: $80,100 First Resistance, Mid-$80k-Range Key to Watch

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on HODL. The spot bitcoin ETF appears attractive, given absolute and relative strength in the past month, while bitcoin’s technical situation has wood to chop, but the February low may indeed be the year’s bottom with sanguine seasonals ongoing.

data.title: JPXN: An Underappreciated Japanese ETF That Ticks Quite A Few Boxes
data.description:
Decent Alpha This Year

The iShares JPX-Nikkei 400 ETF (JPXN), which will complete its silver jubilee on the markets later in October this year, has gotten off to a fairly decent start in 2026. JPXN, which is priced at an expense ratio of 0.48%, is up by a threshold of early double digits and is outperforming global markets by 1.61x.

If you’re curious to better understand this Japanese alpha generator (which admittedly comes across as quite low profile, given that it has only managed AUM of less than $150 million since 2001) and see if it's worth pursuing now, keep reading.

What Does JPXN Do?

Through a process known as representative sampling (rather than owning all stocks from a certain index, an ETF only chooses a sample of stocks that in aggregate mirror the characteristics of an ETF), JPXN attempts to mirror the performance of an interesting index called the JPX-Nikkei Index 400 [JNI400]. Despite resorting to sampling rather than full replication, note that JPXN’s tracking errors are quite tight, showing that it does a good job as a passively managed ETF.

I’ve used the word ‘interesting’ because this index doesn’t just randomly pick a pool of Japanese stocks and weigh them based on their respective market cap or price like most Japanese ETFs (having said that, do consider that JPXN is still dominated by giant and large caps, which account for around 83% of the portfolio). Rather, the goal of this index is to cover a basket of roughly 400 Japanese stocks that score highly on shareholder-friendly policies and profitability metrics. How does it go about building a portfolio of this ilk?

Well, firstly, during the initial weeding-out process, Japanese stocks with less than a 3-year trading history are scrapped. Businesses with an operating loss (deficit) or net loss over the past 3 years are not considered. Likewise, even if they have a net liability position (where liabilities exceed assets) over 3 years, they are not considered.

Once 1000 stocks have made the cut, ranks are assigned based on how well these stocks fare with regard to the 3-year ROE (Return on Equity), 3-year cumulative operating profit, and market capitalization. The first two metrics receive higher weights of 40% each, while the latter receives a weight of 20%. This is quite a contrast to some of the other notable Japanese indices that tend to weigh their constituents on the basis of price or market cap. Note, however, that this isn’t the end of the weighting process.

In the next stage of portfolio building, the intention is to over-index stocks with high corporate governance standards, whereby those that adopt global accounting standards such as the IFRS receive a higher weight. Likewise, businesses with a high proportion of independent directors on their boards as well as female representatives receive a higher score. Also, Japanese companies that provide earnings and corporate governance reports in English receive a higher score. Eventually, the top 400 ranked stocks make it to JNI400.

Is JPXN Worth Pursuing Now?

Yes, I believe JPXN would make for a good buy now; here’s why.

Considering the screening lengths that JNI400 goes to source fundamentally strong stocks with high corporate governance standards that are in keeping with global practices, I feel JPXN could well attract additional interest from the global fund management community.

For an index that resorts to considerable intricacy in its portfolio-building exercise, it’s also interesting to note that it doesn’t resort to a great deal of churn every year; once the stocks are finalized (only 18% of its holdings get turned over during a 1-year time span, well below the 29% rate seen by your standard ETF). This will appeal to those who like to see stability in their long-term holdings, while the ETF won’t also have to incur a lot of transaction costs that could impact returns. Unlike a lot of ETFs that come across as quite concentrated, especially with their top holdings, note that JPXN’s top 10 share is quite well controlled at less than 19%.

Then, economic conditions in Japan look rather encouraging. For instance, take a look at a forward-looking economic proxy called the Leading Economic Index compiled by the Cabinet Office. Since May 2025, it has been convalescing every month and is now at its highest point in nearly 4 years.

Exports, which remain a key cog of the Japanese story too, are seeing very strong momentum, growing annually for 7 straight months (most recently it has been growing at double digits), with the recent figure hitting record highs of 11 trillion Japanese Yen! What I also find quite interesting to note is that Japan has recently opened the room for its defense businesses to now export their lethal arm products (such as missiles and destroyers) to international markets for the first time (previously only non-lethal arm exports were allowed). Entry into this sub-$30 billion-sized global market could give another shot in the arm to Japanese industrials (which account for the lion’s share of this portfolio) and the overall export segment.

Since this is a product that pursues Japanese businesses with good shareholder-friendly practices such as strong payouts, investors can also be reasonably confident of procuring sturdy distributions from JPXN as well. Already historically over the long term (5 or 10 years), we’ve seen that JPXN has been growing its dividends at well over two times the pace of other ETFs, but over the last 3 years or last year alone, the ramp-up in pace of dividend growth has dwarfed other ETFs. Such stellar dividend growth by JPXN shouldn’t come as a surprise, as Japanese businesses are now officially the strongest dividend growers among the major global markets (with some companies even growing their dividends at a pace of 100-300% annually). We now have a scenario where Japanese businesses are paying out an impressive 40% of their profits as dividends, which is even higher than the 34% average payout seen by companies from the S&P 500.

I also like JPXN’s overall technical picture. The image below showcases this product’s weekly movements, and we can see that since bottoming out at the sub-$65 levels in March 2025, the ETF has been on a strong uptrend. However, earlier in February 2026, the euphoria appeared to have gone overboard as JPXN was trading entirely outside its upper Bollinger band (which is over two standard deviations from the 20-week moving average), while the RSI indicator at the lower end of the chart too was flagging overbought conditions.

While that was not a great time to buy, the ETF has since witnessed a healthy retracement back to its 20-weekly moving average, enabling those who missed out on the rally to get in at a more favorable price (the RSI too is no longer flagging overbought conditions).

Meanwhile, the presence of bullish momentum can still be validated, as JPXN is still trading above all its key moving averages on the daily chart, especially the pivotal 200-DMA.

I also feel that JPXN’s investment case is abetted by its potential to benefit from some mean reversion interest, particularly from value-conscious investors who move funds around the globe. To elaborate, on a P/E basis, JPXN is priced at a 9% discount to global stocks, and on a P/B basis, the discount is even more pronounced at 40%.

Given this valuation discount, I could see investors rotating out of expensive global pockets into Japanese equities, thus driving JPXN’s relatively low relative strength ratio closer to its long-term mean of 0.83x (currently there is a 25% gap from the mean).

Risks

Japan’s inflation appears to be rather well-controlled and has been on a declining trend for 4 straight months; however, if the Middle Eastern war isn’t definitely resolved, this narrative could well flip, as 95% of its domestic crude oil requirements come from the Middle East. While Japanese businesses will have to deal with higher costs of raw materials, Japanese consumers too will feel the pain by way of stressed budgets as steeper energy costs leave less room for other discretionary purchases.

As noted earlier, JPXN is not the most popular Japanese ETF around, and this also results in some pretty wide spreads between its bid and ask (0.17% in percentage terms). Thus, investors who pursue this product could be susceptible to slippage risks.

Closing Thoughts

JPXN is recommended as a buy on account of its screening policy that seeks to pick out fundamentally strong Japanese stocks with shareholder-friendly policies, a growing dividend culture in Japan, relatively healthy economic conditions in Japan (with an opening of new markets for Japanese exports), relatively cheap valuations, and encouraging technicals.

data.title: Hartford Growth Opportunities Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

US equities ended lower in a volatile quarter as shifting market sentiment between fears of a prolonged war in Iran and hopes for a quick resolution spurred considerable uncertainty about the path of inflation and economic growth. The US Federal Reserve (Fed) held interest rates unchanged during the quarter, with the Fed's quarterly "dot plot" in March showing only one projected interest-rate cut in 2026. However, Fed Chair Jerome Powell stated that the central bank's outlook for interest rates, inflation, and the labor market was highly uncertain as policymakers grapple with the economic implications of the war in Iran and the inflationary effect of high oil prices. Growth stocks significantly underperformed their value counterparts over the quarter, as investors rotated toward cyclicals and more defensive areas of the market amid anxiety about the disruptive influence of AI on the technology sector, particularly software and data-services companies. Fears spread to private-equity firms and lenders with significant exposure to the software industry, with several funds seeing redemption pressures and limiting withdrawals. Economic data released during the quarter was mixed, but the economy remained on a firm footing. The US Supreme Court struck down President Donald Trump's global tariffs, undercutting his key economic policy and setting the stage for a prolonged legal battle for as much as US$170 billion in tariff refunds for importers. President Trump immediately responded with 10% tariffs on most foreign imports to the US for a period of 150 days.

The Russell 3000 Growth Index returned -9.54% for the period. Within the Index, 7 out of 11 sectors fell over the period. Financials and information technology were the bottom-performing sectors, while energy and consumer staples were the top-performing sectors for the quarter.

Performance Summary

  • The Hartford Growth Opportunities Fund (I Share) underperformed the Russell 3000 Growth Index during the quarter.
  • Sector allocation, a result of the portfolio's bottom-up stock-selection process, was a driver of relative underperformance. Allocation effect was driven by our underweight to industrials and consumer staples, but it was partially offset by our underweight to information technology and financials, and an overweight to energy.
  • Stock selection also detracted from returns. Weak selection in consumer discretionary, healthcare, and real estate was offset by selection in information technology.
  • Top relative detractors from performance during the quarter included overweight positions in DoorDash (consumer discretionary) and KKR (financials) and not owning Costco (consumer staples).

Positioning & Outlook

  • While the market remains fluid, we continue to focus on companies for which we have a more constructive view on future growth and attractive risk rewards. With dispersion elevated and outcomes increasingly driven by company-specific fundamentals, we believe the portfolio is well-positioned for this volatile environment.
  • At the end of the period, our largest overweight was to communication services, and we were most underweight to information technology, relative to the benchmark.

The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For more current performance information to the most recent month ended, please visit Home .

Share Class Inception: A, Y - 2/19/02; F - 2/28/17; I - 8/31/06; R3, R4, R5 - 12/22/06; R6 - 11/7/14. Performance shown prior to the inception of a class reflects performance and operating expenses of another class(es) (excluding sales charges, if applicable). Had fees and expenses of a class been reflected for the periods prior to the inception of that class, performance would be different. Since inception (SI) performance for A is from 3/31/63; and F, I, R3, R4, R5, R6, and Y is from 2/19/02. Performance and expenses for other share classes will vary. Additional information is in the prospectus. Only Class A assesses a sales charge. Performance for periods of less than one year is not annualized.

Benchmark: Russell 3000 Growth Index is an unmanaged index that measures the performance of those Russell 3000 Index companies with higher price-to-book ratios and higher forecasted growth values. Indices are unmanaged and not available for direct investment.

Net expenses reflect such arrangements only with respect to Class I. These arrangements remain in effect through 2/28/27 unless the Fund's Board of Directors approves an earlier termination. Without these arrangements, performance would have been lower.

Important Risks: Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Security prices fluctuate in value depending on general market and economic conditions and the prospects of individual companies. • The securities of large market capitalization companies may underperform other segments of the market. • Mid-cap securities can have greater risks and volatility than large-cap securities. • Growth investing style may go in and out of favor, which may cause the Fund to underperform other equity funds that use different investment styles. • To the extent the Fund focuses on one or more sectors, including the information technology, consumer discretionary and communication services sectors, the Fund may be subject to increased volatility and risk of loss if adverse developments occur. • Because the Fund may hold a limited number of securities, the Fund is subject to a greater risk of loss if any of those securities decline in price. • The Fund may have high portfolio turnover, which could increase its transaction costs and an investor's tax liability. • Foreign investments may be more volatile and less liquid than U.S. investments and are subject to the risk of currency fluctuations and adverse political, economic and regulatory developments.

Investors should carefully consider a fund's investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses. This and other important information is contained in a fund's full prospectus and summary prospectus, which can be obtained by visiting Home . Please read it carefully before investing.

Mutual funds are distributed by Hartford Funds Distributors, LLC (HFD), Member FINRA. Advisory services are provided by Hartford Funds Management Company, LLC (HFMC). Certain funds are sub-advised by Wellington Management Company LLP. HFMC and Wellington Management are SEC registered investment advisers. HFD and HFMC are not affiliated with any sub-adviser.3

data.title: Something Unusual Is Happening Around TLTW That You Need To Know
data.description:
Today I'm coming back to talk about iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond BuyWrite Strategy ETF (TLTW) because something unusual and fascinating has happened. From March to today, the market has changed face both on the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) and therefore on TLTW, and in my opinion, not exactly for the better. With the ceasefire, the probability of an appreciation of TLT has, in my opinion, increased, and confirmation is found in the options market (not exactly good news for TLT). Along with this, the probability of competitive premiums at rolling is also being questioned. In short, a context that seems to scream "wait"! Which in financial language, I, personally, translate as "hold." And I'll explain why, but first...

A Brief Overview of TLTW

I've already discussed this ETF several times, precisely because I find it very interesting in the buy-write landscape. For this reason, before continuing, let me give you a small summary of what, in my opinion, is essential to know:

The fund tracks the performance of the CBOE TLT 2% OTM BuyWrite Index with a low tracking error.

It is therefore structured as a Fund of Funds (invests in TLT + options overlay), maintaining as Main Holding the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) at 100.10% of the portfolio. Then it has an active option (snapshot March 31, 2026) Call TLT strike 89, expiry April 2026, weight -0.25%. An option that will roll monthly, moving the strike. In line with the Buy-Write strategy (covered call). In this case the 2% OTM strike is chosen to balance premium collected vs. probability of being called away. What does this mean? That TLTW has a dynamic strike (102% of close): with markets in trending up markets, the strike "chases" the price, always 2% above, and therefore TLTW will never capture the rally of TLT >2% monthly. This is why the ideal scenario would be a flat TLT or +1% per month. This is because TLTW captures all the upside + premium.

The fund has a TTM yield of 13.49%, of which the 30-Day SEC Yield (March 31, 2026) is at 4.33% (TLT coupon) distributed monthly, meaning that past distributions included return of capital, derived from the premiums of the buy-write strategy.

The 0.05% spread combined with the 0.35% expense ratio brings the true all-in cost to 0.40% annual.

The Logic Behind Price Movements

TLT has a strong sensitivity to rates, given that it is composed of treasuries with twenty-year maturity and long duration (15.31 years).

Indicatively, a 1% move in rates generates a 15.31% inverse move in TLT price. This confirms an extreme sensitivity to rates; with Fed Funds at 20Y yield at 4.85%, a return to 3.5% on 30Y (+135 bps move) generates a +20.7% "theoretical" gain in TLT; conversely a spike to 6% (+115 bps), a 17.6% loss.

Applying this to TLTW means that yields drop; having a strike 2% away from current prices would be, in the reference month, a nice brake on returns. And how can this risk be quantified? I, personally, look at kurtosis and skewness, which today are at 30 days, respectively, 7-8 and 2.5. Clearly, in my opinion, it means there is a much higher than normal probability of extreme events and, with more probability of TLT rallies.

To summarize, the probability that the 2% barrier (upside cap) of TLTW's options strategy breaks (exceeds) increases significantly.

The Logic Behind Distribution

Distribution side, as of April 22, ATM Strike (86.85): Calls closed at an IV ~15-20 and Put IV ~15-20. Why this option specifically? Because the mechanics behind the choice of options are passive. Which doesn't always maximize distribution. Apparently (for example), there were more profitable deep OTM Strikes, considering that Puts with strike at 72 had an IV ~130 and Calls strike at 105 had an IV ~200. So (today), the premium collected is therefore medium-low compared to the available IV range. The same would apply to expiries: selling 30-day maximizes theta decay (faster time decay) but collects less vega (volatility premium) compared to selling 60-90 days; also synonymous with the fact, the way I read it, that the ceasefire has shifted uncertainty from 30 days to above 30 days. This is because today the VIX TLT index (VXTLT) is at 12.84, and its average is between 16 and 18, so it's in the bottom 20% of the historical range, a low premium environment. For example, already in March it was at 38, the top 5% range environment, so TLTW's mechanics already rewarded it more.

To summarize, at monthly renewal, the premiums (if the situation remains unchanged) could be uncompetitive.

My Overall Opinion

After the outbreak of the conflict in Iran, inflation spiked from February's 2.4% to March 2026's 3.3%, along with 1-year break-even inflation exceeding 5% in March. So the common opinion is that the Fed maintains an aggressive tone, and one would therefore think that solutions that sell call options on TLT are excellent (if yields increase, prices drop, and the MOVE keeps premiums high).

But this today would mean completely ignoring the forward-looking nature of the market. The context has changed in the last two weeks of ceasefire: the 1-year break-even inflation [BEI] has dropped below 3%, while the 5- and 10-year ones have remained at 2.5%. Considering inflation today at 3.3%, it means that the market is starting to price in a "monetary normalization." A normalization is well expressed by the options market, which explicitly seems to tell us prices could increase, and the premium could lower at rolling (kurtosis and skewness). So in my opinion, this is not the time to buy TLTW, but rather to hold it (if you have it in your strategy).

Risk

The holding exposes at this moment to underperformance risk of TLT.

With a duration above 15, it means that small variations in yields impact TLT's price in a pronounced manner.

And TLTW fully replicates TLT's performance in price declines and only up to the strike in price increases.

It follows that if the conflict in Iran subsides and the new inflationary expectations are confirmed, Treasury yields normalize, and TLTW's capital gain would be limited to the strike.

On the negative side, if the conflict in Iran continues, inflation would support treasury yields. Prices would drop, hitting TLTW's prices 100%. Unlike TLT, though, TLTW would benefit from a distribution currently 2x higher (would cushion the fall).

Then it will be a matter of seeing at rolling the conditions of the options (in this last scenario, presumably it would be better because this would increase the IV and also shift the uncertainty to the short term, to 30 days; in the first, presumably worse).

Conclusion

To conclude, I think the right rating is HOLD. Today TLTW would be acquired in an uncertain context. The probability that there could be a spike in TLT's price has increased, but TLTW would only cover 2%. Similarly, at the time of rolling (less than 10 days away), if the context remains unchanged, optimized conditions for the premium would not be obtained (at least judging from today's data, less than 10 days away). Considering the uncertainty behind the ceasefire and Trump's imminent decisions, an unfavorable context is created, in my opinion, to take a position now. This naturally does not compromise TLTW's validity, which in my opinion remains a tactical portfolio instrument.

data.title: AMD Finally Found Its Edge
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) has been catching up to Nvidia (NVDA) for the past 5 years.

Some think it will never happen, but personally, I think that time has finally come.

AMD has made major breakthroughs in both software and hardware that make it a viable, and in some cases, better option than Nvidia.

I highlighted in my last article how AMD looked more attractive than Nvidia (NVDA) based on the industry dynamics laid out at GTC.

The release of the ROCm 7 software stack and AMD's lead in deploying TSMC's (TSM) 2nm-class GPUs reinforce this point.

These developments will have deep implications for both the business profile and outlook.

Time To ROCm 7 N’ Roll

It’s a tale as old as time.

Nvidia’s real moat was never hardware; it was CUDA, Nvidia’s software, which created a walled garden around its ecosystem.

But this has changed with the release of ROCm 7.0 and its subsequent updates.

Offering full support for the Instinct MI350 series, improved code portability via HIP 7.0, and also introducing enterprise-focused tools, like AMD Resource Manager and AI Workbench.

Since the first release, we've had a few updates and are now at ROCm 7.2.2, which introduces further support and improved optimization.

Now, I’m no expert on GPU software, but ROCm has been well-received. What I do know, however, is that AMD is making a very good decision by keeping ROCm open-source.

This allows major partners like Meta Platforms (META) and Microsoft (MSFT) to optimize it for their own workloads.

CUDA may still be better for training, but for inference, AMD is giving Nvidia a run for its money.

And the proof is in the numbers. I’d argue that this is a big reason why Meta chose AMD for their historic 6-gigawatt deal.

Nvidia spent decades building a walled garden, and now AMD is providing a credible alternative.

The 2nm Win: A Real But Qualified Lead

AMD’s upcoming MI400 series represents a real step change from what we’ve seen so far. For the first time ever, these chips will be made using TSMC’s 2nm technology.

What does this mean exactly?

This smaller architecture should make AMD chips more efficient and translate into better economies of scale when it comes to deploying data centers.

But we must also acknowledge that Nvidia’s Rubin, which will be released around the same time, is not far behind, even though it used N3 technology, not N2.

Early tests suggest AMD leads in memory capacity and bandwidth, while Nvidia leads on raw compute per chip (based on current disclosures). The two platforms arrive essentially simultaneously.

Here’s a breakdown of what each chip will look like in terms of specs.

Metric

Nvidia Rubin (3nm FinFET)

AMD MI455X (2nm GAAFET)

Process Node

TSMC N3

TSMC N2

FP4 Performance

~50 PFLOPS

~40 PFLOPS

HBM4 Capacity

~192 GB

432 GB

Memory Bandwidth

~13 TB/s

19.6 TB/s

Availability

H2 2026

H2 2026

Source: Authors’ work using Tom’s Hardware data.

Why This Matters

With these developments in mind, there are two key reasons investors should be very bullish.

Gross Margins: AMD has always suffered from lower margins than Nvidia, but that will change soon.

As the data center segment expands, margins should expand towards the mid-60s range, according to analyst estimates.

But I think these could come even higher than expected.

For starters, GPU prices have been exploding higher, and there’s a clear shortage.

But more importantly, AMD is no longer trying to carve itself a place in the market. It is established and arguably leading Nvidia in some metrics, and this gives it more pricing power.

The Platform Multiplier: As ROCm becomes a viable open standard, AMD gains from building its own CUDA-like ecosystem. While the system is open-source, there’s still an incentive for customers to double down on AMD GPUs and CPUs to get the most out of the AMD ecosystem.

AMD Outlook: The Case For +$300

Indeed, the case for a $300 AMD price target ("PT") has never been stronger.

Fundamentally, the margin expansion and EPS outlook should get us there in the next year or so.

Applying a 30x multiplier on 2027 EPS would imply a share price close to $330.

From a technical standpoint, we’ve established a strong double bottom and have officially broken out to new highs, so momentum is very much on the bull's side, with room for the weekly RSI to keep running some more before a meaningful pull-back.

Risks

But there are risks to the thesis.

AMD’s MI455X advantage depends heavily on securing sufficient HBM4 supply, which could certainly be an issue as the memory bottleneck persists.

On top of this, both AMD and Nvidia rely on TSMC’s (TSM) Taiwan-based manufacturing. This introduces concentration corners, too.

TSM could itself face issues, as well as the very well-known geopolitical risk that comes with Taiwan.

Final Thoughts

The way I see it, AMD has been slowly but steadily making the right moves in the AI space, and it’s finally paying off.

I think AMD has room to run to over $300 in the next 12 months, at which point I might reassess the bull case and would consider perhaps taking some profits.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Spirit Airlines Surges As Trump Adminstration Nears Rescue Deal
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Trump administration nears Spirit rescue deal with major ownership stake. (0:16) Boeing tops expectations. (0:45) Aluminum market already in a ‘Black Swan’ shock. (1:41)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, the Trump administration is nearing a deal to rescue Spirit Airlines (FLYYQ).

Shares of Spirit soared after the Wall Street Deal reported that the U.S. government would loan Spirit as much as $500M in return for warrants for a significant stake in the carrier.

Further reports said the government could own up to 90% of Spirit following a rescue deal. The Transportation Department and the Commerce Department are said to be involved talks.

President Donald Trump said on Tuesday he would like to see a buyer for the distressed airline and said he may be open to the government helping out.

Among active stocks, Boeing (BA) is up after reporting quarterly results that topped Wall Street expectations on the top and bottom lines. Investors weighed improving aircraft deliveries against ongoing geopolitical and travel-market risks.

SA Analyst Dhierin Bechai said if “Boeing had been able to deliver Boeing 737s as planned, the operating cash flow would have been positive. So, it was not the best quarter the company could have had, but it still showed substantial improvement."

AT&T (T) is trading defensively as a decline in legacy revenue and pressure on free cash flow overshadowed gains in internet and postpaid phone revenue.

Analyst Samuel Smith said the results reaffirmed his “biggest concerns with AT&T: that it operates in a perpetually high capital intensity business and highly competitive industry that keeps its growth and cash generation constrained.”

And AST SpaceMobile (ASTS) shares is rallying after the company secured commercial authority from the FCC to deliver direct-to-device cellular broadband from space.

In other news of note, the global aluminum ​market is already ⁠experiencing a Black Swan supply shock due to disruptions from the Iran war, which likely will cause a supply shock that will lead to major shortages this year.

Nick Snowdown, the top metals analyst at commodities trader Mercuria, told the FT: "The scale of the supply shock ​we're seeing in the aluminum market is probably the largest single supply shock a base ⁠metals market has suffered in the post-2000 era."

He estimates the market ​will face a minimum deficit of ~2M tons between now and year-end, which may prove conservative, as it assumes a near-term improvement in alumina flows through the Strait of Hormuz.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, Goldman Sachs strategist Tony Pasquariello says the stock market’s primary uptrend remains intact. But he warns that near-term conditions have grown more challenging.

The relative strength index of the S&P is right at overbought levels, and “the most intense spec buying is behind us now, so the short-term setup is certainly more demanding than it was at the end of March,” Pasquariello said.

He laid out a detailed bear case, arguing that “stock operators are complacent” and that any “impingement on the flow of energy is set to cascade” as the Iran conflict continues. He also pointed to “a sharp deceleration in CTA demand” and noted the rally has been “exceptionally narrow” with “only 5 percent of the stocks in the Russell 3000 index (IWV)” trading at 52-week highs.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Intuitive Surgical: Strong Quarter, Mispriced Stock
data.description:
Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) has dropped 8% since I initiated coverage with a "Buy" rating. As a market-leading robotic-assisted surgery platform with a strong margin systems portfolio coupled with high-margin recurring revenues, I believe the company is attractively positioned, but the company is not immune to some transient pressures. The generic bull case of "everybody needs healthcare and life expectancy is increasing" does not cover the full story. In this report, I discuss the company’s earnings, some pressures that the company faces, and update my price target, as I believe the company remains a "Buy."

Intuitive Surgical Sees Revenue Exceeding Procedure Growth

Intuitive Surgical reported sales of $2.77 billion, indicating around 23% growth, exceeding analyst estimates of $2.62 billion in revenues. Systems sales rose 24.5% to $650.7 million, while instrumentation & accessories sales grew 23.3% to $1.69 billion, and services growth was up 19.5% to $433.7 million. Gross profit increased 25.6% to $1.83 billion, indicating a 140 basis point margin expansion to 66.1%. Operating expenses grew 10.9% to $975.2 million, driven by higher SG&A, leading operating income to grow 48% to $855.3 million with margins climbing 520 basis points to 30.9%. The top-line growth exceeded the 17% growth in procedures, which drives service and consumables revenues.

We note that margins of the da Vinci 5 robotic surgery system, which is the flagship of the company, now are comparable with the da Vinci Xi, and margins for the Ion robotic bronchoscopy system, targeting lung biopsy procedures, are climbing towards the company composite margin. In other words, the margins are set to become accretive to the composite margins in the near term. Earnings per share were $2.28 and $2.50 on a non-GAAP basis, exceeding analyst estimates by $0.39. So, it was a good quarter with sales and system growth, gross margin improvement driven by volume and maturing margins coupled with operating cost growth below revenue growth.

KPIs Updated For Intuitive Surgical

Intuitive Surgical does not provide detailed financial guidance, but it does provide guidance on operating metrics that underpin earnings and revenue growth. The number of procedures is now expected to grow 13.5%-15.5% year-on-year, indicating a 50 basis point increase. Gross margins have been lifted from 67%-68% to 67.5%-68.5%. This includes a reduction in tariff impacts from 120 basis points to 100 basis points. At the same time, we note that due to oil costs and increasing inflation, there is some cost risk on logistics, while semiconductor memory costs also provide a cost risk. Operating expenses are expected to grow 11%-14%. It does seem that each 100 basis point growth in procedures drives 135 basis points in recurring revenues, and that puts Intuitive Surgical in a good position to continue expanding its operating leverage.

We do note that there are some headwinds. In China, low tender activity and competition are putting pressure on the results, while low placements in Japan are weighing on procedure growth, with the financials of hospitals in Japan being a weakness. In the US, GLP-1 surgeries that have a net effect of eating less and losing weight were under pressure, and people are turning to GLP-1 drugs such as Ozempic. Other risks include the sensitivity of healthcare equipment CapEx to macroeconomic outlooks.

So, it was a good quarter, and 2026 is looking good for now, but there are definitely some risk factors to navigate for Intuitive Surgical.

Intuitive Surgical Stock Looks Attractive

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Intuitive Surgical, my price target is $584.50, which is more or less unchanged from my prior target of $584.50. We do note that this price target is based on the earnings in 2025 against the elevated median EV/EBITDA of 46.1x. Given the company’s position in the market and its business model, I do believe this premium is more than justified, and we also see that the company exited 2025 in a similar range, but right now the earnings that are already realized are not even priced in. If we were to price in the earnings for 2026, we would end up with 49% upside. Earnings estimates have not changed significantly, with no change in EBITDA and a 2.7% backloaded lift in free cash flow. The company is expected to have a CAGR of 14% on sales and EBITDA and 29% growth on free cash flow.

For this year, the margins are expected to dip as programs are ramping up, but we are seeing good progress on the margin improvement, and that paves the way for margin improvements in 2027 and 2028. Free cash flow growth is expected to be robust with strong cash conversion. The company is in a net cash position even after returning 75% of its free cash flow to shareholders in the form of share repurchases.

Conclusion: Intuitive Surgical Has Some Risks, But The Business Model Is Robust

There is definitely some risk for Intuitive Surgical in the form of higher input costs, softness in China and Japan, and potential pressure on healthcare equipment CapEx. That may affect the company’s growth profile in the near term, as the placement of new equipment does not only boost system sales but also recurring revenues from consumables, parts, and services. However, as a market leader, even with competitors entering the robot-assisted surgery market, I do believe that Intuitive Surgical can maintain a competitive edge. As a result, I am maintaining my "Buy" rating.

data.title: EQT Posted A Giant Quarterly Comparison As Expected
data.description:
EQT (EQT) corporation just had a winter first quarter that is likely to be hard to beat for some years to come. Naturally the cold La Niña helped things along. But that hardly got mentioned as management "patted itself on the back" for that excellent first quarter. To be sure, management deserves at least some recognition for what happened (just not all of it), as the last article mentioned. Those operating improvements will last a lot longer than a cold winter. Not only that, but continuing operating improvements will likely provide downside protection when the inevitable pricing decrease part of the business cycle begins. This management has long had a growth mentality that never stops looking for ways to grow the business (and profits).

This company has minimal exposure to liquids. Therefore, it does not benefit as much from the Strait of Hormuz situation as oil companies do. But there is the rising exportability of North America. As that export ability rises, the price of natural gas could (at some point in the future) join world market pricing that is generally much stronger than the United States or even North America's natural gas prices in general. That provides yet another avenue of profit and cash flow expansion if it happens.

First Quarter

The first quarter got a big boost from the weather and operational improvements to post one of the larger first quarter comparisons we may ever see for this company.

Keep in mind that earnings are really a small percentage of revenue. Also, it needs to be remembered that costs are paid for (in a very large part) at the lower level of prices shown (elsewhere in the press earnings release in much more detail) in the quarter for the past fiscal year (shown above). Therefore, the increase in the commodity prices that resulted in a much higher realized price (for the current fiscal year) largely heads straight to the bottom line with only a few deductions.

The result, of course, was that earnings per share for the first quarter nearly doubled (as adjusted) or did way better than that with the GAAP-reported numbers. A similar situation happened with the GAAP cash flow number even though the percentage gain was lower.

Management receives a whole lot of credit for tightly controlling controllable expenses in the comparison in the earnings press release. The tight management demonstrated is so rarely seen when it comes to execution.

Cash Flow

Similarly, the free cash flow was at a level that may not be seen in the first quarter for some years to come.

Note that the cash flow shown here was equal to the cash flow earned for all of fiscal year 2022. That is a big difference that one does not often see even in the commodity business (volatile as commodity prices are). There are many times when a measurement moves from negative to positive in a big way. But rarely does it go from positive to a whole lot more positive. When I worked in a commodity industry, that was our wish (and our idea of perfection).

Tight FCF Management

Another big thing that management did was redirect that free cash flow influx to repay debt.

Note that this management did not wait for a cash windfall. That is a very common trap in this industry. The unpredictable nature of commodity prices nearly always dictates massive deleveraging FAST.

Now, once the debt was at acceptable levels, then relying upon commodity prices and routine cash flows was acceptable. That is especially true for a company like this that has considerable midstream presence to minimize some of the upstream earnings volatility.

But again, this is very good management that gets total credit for taking advantage of all that cash to adjust debt levels when it can (like now). Yes, management got lucky. But that luck was an "extra" it was not a necessity. It is very clear that management controlled the "necessary part" a while back so it was out of the picture ASAP.

This is the difference between excellent management and what I often see. This management keeps the uncontrollable to a minimum so that it does not threaten the corporation.

Global Market

Preliminary reports suggest that the global LNG market will be tightening for some time to come.

The caveat with something like this is that production tends (to come out of the woodwork to change after the Iranian situation (or any challenge like this) settles down and a clearer picture emerges. Everything from faster-than-expected repairs to unexpected production increases, all of which are not apparent right now, can come into play to change that long-term picture.

While it may be an opportunity, it is also a moving target.

Real-Life Example

This management mentioned many times that they had no interest in exporting (anything at all) because they viewed the world markets as likely to be weak due to large increases in exporting ability throughout the world.

Even good managements have plans that "go out the window" in this extremely volatile and low-visibility market. As shown with the slide before, clearly that period of slack pricing changed with the Iranian situation. No one saw it coming either.

But this is what happens when you have to make long-term plans in a very volatile and low-visibility industry. A fair part of those plans "turn left" as shown above.

What you have to do as management is do less damage than you do good. This management does far more good than it does damage. That is the best that you can hope for.

Summary

EQT got a lot of help from Mother Nature to post a great first quarter comparison that one seldom sees. But management receives a lot of credit for tight cost management, a growth attitude, and cash flow management. Altogether, this remains a strong buy idea in that management is doing the right things to keep the company growing for the foreseeable future.

That "strong buy" is not a statement about where the stock price will go in the immediate future because almost any commodity stock price can move 20% "because it's Tuesday." No sound reason is needed. Volatility is the name of the game.

Instead, what it does mean is that in the long run the stock price is likely still reasonable given prospects that lie ahead and that this management is doing the right things (and will likely continue to do the right things). The growth strategy of this company likely provides considerable downside protection.

Risks

Should management ever change that growth strategy that it has relentlessly pursued ever since it took control of the company, I would want to re-evaluate my investment regardless of whether I was winning or losing.

For me, managements that do the right thing and, by implication, will continue to do the right thing are far more important than a whole lot of other things I consider. Management is often (by far) the most valuable asset or liability that is not on the balance sheet. I therefore look for above-average management (as is clearly the case here) before I consider anything else.

This is an investment-grade idea. Therefore, a severe and sustained commodity price downturn is likely to affect other companies far more than this one.

This company has significant interests in midstream operations. That can mitigate at least somewhat the volatility of upstream operations. However, those midstream operations need to pull their weight, or a spinoff would be advisable.

data.title: FNDB: A Bet On Valuation Mean Reversion In 2026
data.description:
The Schwab Fundamental U.S. Broad Market ETF (FNDB) is a bet on mean reversion in relative valuation multiples. Its broad diversification across 1,600+ stocks with a fundamental (rather than market cap) weighting approach could be valuable in 2026. Given overheated growth in the US stock market, FNDB’s sizable tilt to the value factor by overweighting cheaper, profitable companies could prove as its strength if high valuations come into focus and inflation remains elevated this year.

FNDB
Name Schwab Fundamental US Broad Market ETF
Management Style Passive
Expense ratio 0.25%
Dividend Yield 1.52%
Assets $1.22B
Average Market Cap $112.6B
Morningstar Rating Silver
Number of Holdings 1,654
Top Sector Technology (18%)
Top 10 Concentration 19%
Portfolio Turnover 11%

Source: Seeking Alpha and Morningstar

Why FNDB Is Different From Other Large Value ETFs

FNDB tracks the performance of the RAFI Fundamental High Liquidity US All Index. This index is based on contrarian investing that gives a fundamental value tilt. The upshot is this. If we take any popular index like the S&P 500 or the Nasdaq-100, they weight their holdings by the market capitalization. But, the economics research argues that the market cap is not necessarily a good measure for a fair value. This can lead to overweighting overvalued and underweighting undervalued companies.

There was a paper written on fundamental weighting by Arnott, Hsu, and Moore in 2005. They showed that fundamental weighting can lead to better performance in developed markets on an absolute and risk adjusted basis. FNDB uses three equally-weighted fundamental metrics to sort stocks:

  1. Adjusted Sales (5-year average multiplied by the average equity over average assets).
  2. Retained Operating Cash flow (5-year average of operating cash flow - dividends - buybacks).
  3. Dividends plus buybacks (5-year average).

After applying liquidity filters, over 1,600 stocks get selected and weighted by their fundamental size. There are several very important implications from this selection process.

Fundamentals Drive FNDB's Weighting

First, FNDB holds almost all major names including big tech stocks like Microsoft, Meta, Apple and Nvidia. However, the weight it gives these companies is highly dependent on their fundamentals. For instance, here are the top 10 holdings:

We see the usual large-cap stocks. But, Nvidia and Tesla are conspicuously missing from the top 10 list. They are buried way under with less than 0.5% allocations. As stock prices for these two companies skyrocketed, their market cap has grown much faster than their fundamentals. Hence, the fund underweights them. In other words, FNDB does not go after price. Rather, it keeps its allocations tied to tangible economic footprints like revenues, cash flows and shareholders’ returns.

FNDB’s Diverse Market Cap

The second implication from this is that FNDB looks at all US stocks as long as they meet its liquidity filter. For this reason, the fund holds way more mid- and small-cap equities, according to Morningstar.

Relative to its large value category, FNDB has material exposure to smaller stocks. But, it is still small. If more small value exposure desired, I wrote an article on AVUV ETF that does an excellent job for this purpose.

FNDB’s Material Value Exposure

But most importantly, the fund tilts heavily towards the value factor. However, its value exposure is very different from other funds like Vanguard Value Index Fund ETF (VTV) that I recently analyzed. VTV uses an extensive model based on 11 factors to separate growth from value equities. Factors like sales and earnings growth rates, P/B, P/S and other things come into play. Like many other large value funds, VTV excludes from its portfolio anything classified as a growth stock.

Conversely, FNDB does something different. It does not apply fundamental filters to exclude stocks. Instead, the fund takes a more agnostic view instead. For this reason, it holds way more stocks in its expansive portfolio than a typical large value fund.

This gives FNDB more exposure to a wide variety of stocks that may give it an edge under certain scenarios. The most important factor is when fundamentals take center stage and valuations multiples revert to their mean. The fund takes a counter-cyclical stance and wins when growth normalizes and its valuations compress. All of this takes time for a broad market to catch up to these developments.

Conversely, FNDB can benefit here because, by its nature, it underweights momentum darlings that have appreciated a lot. For instance, when concerns around growth surfaced at the end of 2025, the performance diverged between FNDB vs. the S&P 500 and even the Nasdaq-100.

But, the fund has one shortcoming. It considers the 5-year averages of companies’ fundamentals. While fundamentals improvement or deterioration is usually a slow-motion process, it can happen rather quickly. There is some risk for FNDB to get caught holding value traps whose financial performance nosedives in a matter of one or two years. That’s one of the tradeoffs that an FNDB’s investor must grapple with.

FNDB's Sector Allocations

By its design, FNDB will still gravitate towards companies that have the largest sales, cash flows and payouts. Currently, those would be large-cap stocks in the technology sector. But, its overall sector allocations would be different from its peers. For instance, here is how the fund compares to the large value category:

Most importantly, FNDB underweights financial stocks and overweights tech and communications sectors. The communications sector is where tech stocks like Meta and Alphabet reside. This provides FNDB with important exposure to growth that many other large value funds lack or don’t have by their design. In this sense, FNDB can have a leg-up should growth stocks rally again in 2026.

FNDB Historic Performance

On a historic basis, FNDB showed very strong performance in an absolute or risk-adjusted basis relative to the large value category.

YTD 1-Year 3-Year 5-Year 10-Year
FNDB 8.9% 36.5% 18.8% 12.7% 13.5%
Large Value Category 6.7% 29.0% 15.5% 10.3% 11.0%

Source: Morningstar

The performance edge is material and visible across all time periods. Not only that, FNDB showed strong volatility-adjusted numbers too. Interestingly, FNDB’s standard deviation was not too different from its peers even though the fund has more exposure to growth and small-cap stocks.

3 Years 5 Years 10 Years
Sharpe Ratio Standard Deviation Sharpe Ratio Standard Deviation Sharpe Ratio Standard Deviation
FNDB 0.93 12.44 0.59 14.94 0.71 15.93
Large Value Category 0.75 12.36 0.47 14.48 0.59 15.43

Source: Morningstar

For instance, if we compare FNDB to VTV and Schwab US Large-Cap Value ETF (SCHV), we see more or less the same performance till about 2020.

But since 2020, the divergence became clear and FNDB's performance edge has been growing. A lot of it has to do with that agnostic view FNDB casts on the total stock market. It does not exclude names with growth profiles. Instead, it assigns them weights depending on what each equity deserves based on its fundamentals.

As a result, FNDB naturally has more exposure to big names in the technology sector that have appreciated a lot since 2020. That has been FNDB's hidden edge over its peers. In this sense, FNDB does provide value exposure with optionality for capturing price appreciation from growth equities. If growth continues doing well, FNDB could outperform its large value peers.

What To Expect From FNDB In 2026

But, the biggest question is: what is in store for FNDB in 2026? The way the Iran conflict plays out, inflation is coming back in the US. The CPI rate spiked to 3.3% in March. Christopher Waller, the Fed governor known for his dovish stance, shifted his rhetoric in favor of "wait and see" after the beginning of the Middle Eastern conflict.

The longer the Strait of Hormuz remains shut, the more damage and inflationary pressures become visible. The main risk is for rising oil prices to make their way into the broad economy via various inputs like fertilizers, transportation and more.

Under the rising inflation scenario, there is a high probability that value can do better than growth in 2026. Under stress, the market may continue zooming on fundamentals. Many market analysts forecast no interest rate cuts in 2026 and, an interest cut pause can put valuations of high flying equities under stress. These circumstances favor value exposure just like it did in 2022-2023 when inflation spiked and growth concerns mounted. FNDB outperformed the Nasdaq-100 and the S&P 500 back then.

Of course, if AI fears subside and growth roars back, FNDB will lag the overall market. But, it will do better than its large value peers. That will be thanks to its somewhat above-average exposure to tech and cyclical names. Its exposure to Apple, Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, Intel and Meta stands at ~14%. These names are largely absent in large value ETFs. Plus, some of its holdings like Microsoft trade at relatively lower multiples to their own history. This can benefit FNDB if growth rally comes back and multiples revert to their means.

Investors Takeaways

FNDB provides useful exposure to the value factor. But, the way it does so is different relative to other large value ETFs. Instead of filtering out stocks completely, FNDB takes a broader view by weighting its holdings using fundamentals. This allows it to spread its bets across a wide universe of stocks and get a little bit of exposure to growth.

Historically, FNDB did the best when investors cared about fundamentals and mean reversion occurred. Inflationary risks are growing in 2026. This may shift investors’ focus to fundamentals and value again. But, even if we hit the middle ground and inflation turns out to be transitory, FNDB will still do better than the majority of its large value peers thanks to its above-average exposure to growth.

data.title: Realty Income: 3 New Reasons This Is Still A Hard Pass
data.description:
I last discussed Realty Income (O) in March, 2025. The stock has underperformed the SP500 (SPY) slightly through that period.

The reasons I noted for why Realty Income should underperform the market are contained in my article.

  • Realty Income: Three Reasons This Is Not A Buy

What's worse, Realty Income has stagnated for the last 3, 5, and 10 year periods, beaten by the SP500 in every category except YTD.

In reviewing the company again, I couldn't help but be underwhelmed by the company's prospects and unenthused by its current valuation. Following up on my original article, I'd like to highlight another 3 reasons why Realty Income remains a sell for my personal wealth-building journey.

1. Growth Can't Match the SP500

The SP500 is expected to grow earnings this year by 22%, next year by 17%, and 2028 by 20%. See below, circled in red.

Even with a fall in SP500 valuations to Liberation Day lows of 21x earnings, that still represents a 12.1% CAGR for 2.5 years. Should valuations remain where they've been since the start of the COVID-19 episode, the CAGR for the SP500 would be 16.8% for 2.5 years.

Over at Realty Income, the story could not be more subdued. Analysts expect 3% growth for the next three years. At current valuations and adding in the dividend, that's amounts to an 8% CAGR for 2.5 years. In an optimistic scenario where valuations return to those seen in the ZIRP era (Zero Interest Rate Policy, roughly 2008-2019) of 18.5x (compared to the current 15x), the CAGR for Realty Income shareholders would be 15.7% for 2.5 years.

What this means is that the most optimistic valuation scenario for Reality Income is worse than a notably pessimistic valuation scenario for the SP500.

2. Margins Are Not Exemplary, and Somewhat False

Let's compare Realty Incomes AFFO margins with those of their competitors, and than that of the SP500. Below are 5 main REITs covered often on Seeking Alpha and known well.

Realty Income (O) Terreno Realty (TRNO) Prologis (PLD) Agree Realty (ADC) NNN REIT (NNN)
AFFO/Revenue, TTM 69% 47% 52% 72% 71%
Valuation 15 32.3 31.2 18.1 13
Total Growth to Dec 2028 9.3% 23.6% 28.3% 14.7% 8.2%

As you can see, no metric stands out as spectacular with regards to Realty Income. It's cheap, but it's not growing well. Its margins are higher than industrial REITs, but lower than closer competitors ADC and NNN. In short, there is no "seven moons aligning" required for us to produce significant alpha.

Where the real divergence in quality and value comes is in comparison, again, to the SP500, keeping in mind that this is the standard to beat. But before I cover the SP500, let's chat about those "AFFO" figures.

AFFO is considered the recurring, adjusted free cashflow of the company. It's a standard marker of "earnings" for REITs, because adding back in large depreciation costs helps us understand more closely what the company can actually earn.

The problem: it doesn't account for net purchases of properties; it only accounts for maintenance capex. If we're going to fully devalue the capex of "megacap" tech stocks like the Mag 7 and look only at their true free cashflows, then we should be fully devalue the capex of Realty Income as well. If we do that, we see that Realty income invests more capital into its business than it pulls out: subtracting net property purchases from levered FCF in the TTM gets you $-1.3B in losses in the TTM. In fact, Realty Income has always bought more property than it has gained in AFFO.

Q4 reported figures were difficult to find on Realty Income's 43-slide Q4 investor presentation. The presentation details "who they are", their stability by design, fantastic its moat is, how diversified its tenant base is by industry and geography, their scaling potential and their expanding TAMs. Finally, despite all this, on page 40 we find that stated AFFO grew 2.1% year-over-year. The company's net debt to adjusted EBITDA is 5.5x, meaning there isn't debt-fueled growth left unless Realty is willing to sacrifice its A- credit rating.

The company issued $2.38B of common stock in the TTM, and bonds totaling $1.35B in the TTM. Realty Income is effectively profitless, because as I mentioned, we standard accounting subtracts ALL capex to arrive at free cashflow. It simply looks profitable because 1) AFFO does not subtract all capex; 2) Realty Income fills in the gaps with bonds and share issuance (outside stimulus). So it can look like Realty Income is a profitable, high margin business - after all, AFFO margin is a whopping 69% - but under the hood, it's pretty rickety. Most REITs do operate this way, but it still needs repeating.

Now lets look at the GAAP profit margins of the SP500, which do largely discount the capex of the Mag 7.

While the SP500 margins are objectively lower than stated margins at Realty Income, this set of businesses has materially improved in quality over the long haul, from debt-ridden, uninspired juggernauts in the late 20th century, to high-margin, contracted revenue, high cash "apex spawners", as superinvestor Mohnish Pabrai would say. This includes companies like Alphabet (GOOGL), which as created notable side businesses for its immense and captive audience of billions of Android users, and Meta Platforms (META), with additions WhatsApp, Threads and smartglasses as budding advertising and platform-based potential revenue streams of the future. These are the companies as part of the SP500 competing against Realty Income for your capital.

Over the long term, the SP500's margins can be expected to rise as dominant high margin companies continue to take over; its revenue can be expected to be further client-contracted as opposed to cyclical; its concentration in quality is expected to grow as weaker businesses give way to the increasing market capitalizations of stronger businesses. These are structural advantages a landowner like Realty Income can not reproduce.

3. Realty Income's Tenants Aren't Even SP500 Quality

Perhaps Realty Income's claim to quality could reside in the quality of its tenants. See below.

I decided to get a hard figure out of these metrics.

I took the credit scores of these 20 companies. I assigned a number, from 1-20, where 20 represents an AAA credit rating, while 1 represents a C credit rating. I then multiplied this by the weight of these tenants in Realty Income's portfolio (for example, 3.3% for 7-Eleven). This gave me a weighted average credit rating for the top 20 tenants of Realty Income. See below.

Conclusion

I have laid out three reasons why Realty Income doesn't meet my standard for investing to beat the SP500.

  1. The company's growth is almost nonexistent compared to the SP500;
  2. The company's true margins, counting capital expenditures, is negative, versus a very positive and growing SP500 profit margin;
  3. The roughly top third of the tenants of the company have a credit rating eight levels below the equivalent of the SP500.

It's for these three reasons, and the reasons listed in my original article, that Realty Income remains uninvestable to me. Further, with the recent price run-up, I'm rating this company a Sell.

Realty Income likely meets my parameters for a Quintuple Vortex. Readers looking for more hard looks at high debt, slow growth, dilutive companies should consider my article Enbridge: "Quintuple Vortex" Exemplified, where this term is explained, and see for themselves through the example of Enbridge (ENB) whether Realty Income is at this level of risk.
